_doc.purpose: Per-finding rationale catalog for sarge reports. report.sh joins this with assessment results by check_id to render the Findings section.
_doc.naming_convention: <NIST-FAMILY>-<CONTROL-NUMBER>-<short-slug>. Family + control are uppercase (e.g. AC-3); slug is lowercase-kebab. Examples: AC-3-secrets-perm, CM-7-cups-running, IA-5-pwquality-minlen. IDs are stable — once published do not rename without a migration note.

schema.family: Long name of the NIST 800-53 Rev 5 family + control (e.g. 'AC-3 — Access Enforcement').
schema.what: Short description of the observed condition. Templates may include shell-style placeholders like {value} that report.sh leaves as-is — the finding 'description' from the check script is what actually appears in reports; this 'what' is reference-only.
schema.why: 2-3 sentences explaining the security risk. References the NIST 800-53 Rev 5 control by name.
schema.scope: "host" (default) or "agent" — agent-scoped findings probe the OpenClaw / agent-runtime overlay and are excluded in --host-only mode.

_doc.coverage: Every fail "..." and warn "..." callsite in assessment/checks/check-*.sh. PASS/SKIP do not need entries.

AC-2-empty-password.what: Local account(s) found with empty password fields in /etc/shadow
AC-2-empty-password.expected: All accounts have a password set or are locked
AC-2-empty-password.why: An empty password field allows login with no authentication, defeating identity assurance entirely. NIST 800-53 AC-2 requires the organization to manage account credentials and disable inactive or improperly provisioned accounts. Empty-password accounts are usually leftover service accounts or misconfigured users that an attacker can pivot through trivially.
AC-2-empty-password.fix: sudo passwd -l <username>  # lock the account, or set a password with: sudo passwd <username>

AC-2-uid-zero.what: Non-root account(s) found with UID 0 in /etc/passwd
AC-2-uid-zero.why: UID 0 is the kernel's superuser — any account assigned UID 0 is root, regardless of name. NIST 800-53 AC-2 requires authoritative account inventory; shadow root accounts bypass auditing because logs key off the username while privileges key off the UID. Attackers create such accounts to persist through password rotations on the canonical root.
AC-2-uid-zero.fix: sudo usermod -u <new-uid> <username>  # assign a non-zero UID, or remove the account: sudo userdel <username>

AC-3-openclaw-dir-perm.why: The ~/.openclaw directory holds workspace state, agent memory, and credential paths used by OpenClaw subprocesses. NIST 800-53 AC-3 requires enforcement of approved access — group/world-readable workspace directories let other local users enumerate which agents and accounts the operator runs. Even read access leaks operational intent.

AC-3-secrets-dir-perm.why: Group/world-readable secrets directory exposes OAuth tokens and API keys cached by OpenClaw to other local users or processes. NIST 800-53 AC-3 requires access enforcement that limits sensitive resources to authorized identities. Any unprivileged local process — including a compromised browser or editor — can read tokens and impersonate the operator against external services.

AC-3-secret-file-perm.what: Individual secret file under ~/.openclaw/secrets is not 600
AC-3-secret-file-perm.why: Even if the parent directory is locked down, lax file modes on individual tokens allow exfiltration via backup, sync, or container bind-mount. NIST 800-53 AC-3 requires per-resource access enforcement, not just directory-level. A 644 token survives a tarball or rsync that preserves modes but loses parent-dir context.

AC-6-passwordless-sudo.what: Current user has passwordless sudo (NOPASSWD) enabled
AC-6-passwordless-sudo.expected: sudo requires a password (no NOPASSWD for this user)
AC-6-passwordless-sudo.why: Passwordless sudo turns any code execution as the user — a malicious script, a hijacked editor plugin, a curl|bash mistake — into immediate root. NIST 800-53 AC-6 requires least privilege, including the principle that elevation must require an explicit re-authentication. Cron and CI runners sometimes need NOPASSWD for narrow commands; an interactive operator account should not.
AC-6-passwordless-sudo.fix: Edit /etc/sudoers.d/ entries with 'sudo visudo' and remove NOPASSWD: from the operator user, or scope it to specific commands.

AC-6-user-in-sudo-group.what: Current user is a member of the 'sudo' group
AC-6-user-in-sudo-group.expected: Service/operator accounts that don't need elevation should not be in 'sudo'
AC-6-user-in-sudo-group.why: Membership in 'sudo' confers blanket root capability subject only to password challenge. NIST 800-53 AC-6 calls for separation of duties between operational and administrative accounts. If this account runs OpenClaw, agents, or daemons day-to-day, a compromise of the agent shell becomes a compromise of root.
AC-6-user-in-sudo-group.fix: If a separate admin account exists: sudo deluser <username> sudo  # otherwise create a dedicated admin account first

AC-17-ufw-inactive.what: UFW firewall is installed but not active
AC-17-ufw-inactive.why: Without an active host firewall, any service that binds to an external interface is reachable from the network. NIST 800-53 AC-17 requires controls on remote access paths. An inactive firewall means the host depends entirely on perimeter or upstream protection — fine in some deployments, but invisible drift if you assumed UFW was enforcing.
AC-17-ufw-inactive.fix: sudo ufw enable  # review rules first with: sudo ufw status verbose

AC-17-ufw-not-installed.expected: UFW or another host firewall (nftables, firewalld) is in place
AC-17-ufw-not-installed.why: Sarge can only attest to UFW; without it, host-level network access enforcement is unverified. NIST 800-53 AC-17 requires documented controls on remote access. If you use nftables or rely on a cloud security group, that's fine — but Sarge cannot confirm it, so this is flagged as a gap until you install UFW or document the alternative.
AC-17-ufw-not-installed.fix: sudo apt install ufw && sudo ufw enable  # or document the alternative firewall in baseline/

AC-17-external-ports.what: Externally-listening TCP ports detected (not bound to loopback)
AC-17-external-ports.expected: Only intentionally-exposed services listen on non-loopback addresses
AC-17-external-ports.why: Every externally-bound port is an attack surface. NIST 800-53 AC-17 requires that remote access points be authorized, monitored, and protected. Many drift incidents start with a service the operator forgot was exposed — a debug port, a leftover dev container, a default-config Postgres.
AC-17-external-ports.fix: Review the listed ports with 'ss -tlnp' and either bind to 127.0.0.1, firewall the port, or document it as an intended remote service.

AU-2-auditd-not-running.expected: auditd is active and persisting events
AU-2-auditd-not-running.why: Without auditd, security-relevant kernel and file events aren't recorded, so post-incident forensics rely entirely on application logs and journald (which can be tampered with by root). NIST 800-53 AU-2 requires the system to generate audit records for defined events; auditd is the standard Linux mechanism. No auditd = no defensible timeline.
AU-2-auditd-not-running.fix: sudo apt install auditd && sudo systemctl enable --now auditd

AU-2-journald-inactive.expected: systemd-journald running, or syslog explicitly configured as substitute
AU-2-journald-inactive.why: journald collects logs from systemd units and the kernel; without it (or syslog), service logs are lost between restarts. NIST 800-53 AU-2 requires consistent audit event capture. A host with no journald and no syslog has no audit trail for daemon failures or security warnings.
AU-2-journald-inactive.fix: sudo systemctl enable --now systemd-journald  # or verify rsyslog/syslog-ng is configured

AU-2-journal-not-persisted.what: journald appears to not be persisting logs to disk
AU-2-journal-not-persisted.why: When journald runs in volatile (RAM-only) mode, all logs vanish on reboot. NIST 800-53 AU-2 expects audit records to survive long enough to support incident response. A reboot during an attack erases the evidence.
AU-2-journal-not-persisted.fix: sudo sed -i 's/^#\?Storage=.*/Storage=persistent/' /etc/systemd/journald.conf && sudo systemctl restart systemd-journald

AU-9-audit-log-bad-owner.why: Audit logs owned by a non-root user can be tampered with by that user, defeating non-repudiation. NIST 800-53 AU-9 requires audit information to be protected from unauthorized modification. If the audit log owner can write to it, the audit log can be edited to hide actions.

AU-9-audit-log-perm.why: World/group-readable audit logs leak sensitive event metadata (commands run, files accessed, user activity) to any local account. NIST 800-53 AU-9 requires that audit information be protected from unauthorized read as well as write. This is also a covert-channel risk: a low-privilege process can fingerprint admin behaviour.

AU-9-audit-log-missing.what: auditd is running but /var/log/audit/audit.log does not exist
AU-9-audit-log-missing.expected: audit.log present at the configured location
AU-9-audit-log-missing.why: auditd is daemonized but no log file exists at the standard path — likely a misconfiguration in /etc/audit/auditd.conf, or audit events being routed elsewhere. NIST 800-53 AU-9 cannot be enforced if the audit sink is unknown. This is a silent-failure mode where operators believe they have audit coverage but don't.
AU-9-audit-log-missing.fix: Check 'log_file' in /etc/audit/auditd.conf and review with: sudo aureport -t

AU-12-no-openclaw-rules.expected: auditctl -l shows watch rules for the secrets directory
AU-12-no-openclaw-rules.why: Without watch rules, reads/writes to high-value secret material are not recorded. NIST 800-53 AU-12 requires that audit records be generated for defined auditable events — for sarge's threat model, OpenClaw secret access is one of those events. Detecting credential exfiltration after the fact requires this trail.
AU-12-no-openclaw-rules.fix: Run: scripts/harden-auditd.sh  # or add: sudo auditctl -w ~/.openclaw/secrets -p rwxa -k openclaw-secrets

AU-12-no-auth-rules.expected: Watch rules on the auth-critical files
AU-12-no-auth-rules.why: These files define who can log in and who can elevate. NIST 800-53 AU-12 expects audit generation for changes to authentication and authorization data. Unmonitored writes to /etc/sudoers are a classic persistence vector.
AU-12-no-auth-rules.fix: sudo auditctl -w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity && sudo auditctl -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity && sudo auditctl -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k identity

CM-2-no-baseline.expected: Baseline file present in repo to anchor drift detection
CM-2-no-baseline.why: Without a documented baseline, every change looks new and there's no anchor for what 'good' looks like. NIST 800-53 CM-2 requires maintaining a current baseline configuration of the information system. For sarge specifically, the baseline file is what subsequent runs compare against to detect drift in the OpenClaw layout.
CM-2-no-baseline.fix: Restore baseline/openclaw.json.baseline from the sarge repo, or regenerate from a known-good host.

CM-6-unattended-not-installed.expected: unattended-upgrades installed and configured for security updates
CM-6-unattended-not-installed.why: Without unattended-upgrades, security patches require manual intervention and tend to lag. NIST 800-53 CM-6 (and SI-2 / Flaw Remediation) require timely application of vendor-released fixes. Background auto-patching narrows the window between disclosure and exploitation.
CM-6-unattended-not-installed.fix: sudo apt install unattended-upgrades && sudo dpkg-reconfigure -plow unattended-upgrades

CM-6-unattended-not-configured.what: unattended-upgrades is installed but the auto-reboot setting in 50unattended-upgrades is not configured
CM-6-unattended-not-configured.why: Installed-but-unconfigured leaves you believing patches are applied when in fact the policy file is incomplete. NIST 800-53 CM-6 requires documented and enforced configuration. Verify the file contents match your maintenance window — kernel updates without auto-reboot still leave the host on the old vulnerable kernel.
CM-6-unattended-not-configured.fix: Edit /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/50unattended-upgrades and confirm Automatic-Reboot, Allowed-Origins, and email settings match policy.

CM-6-pending-updates-low.what: Small number of package updates pending (1-5)
CM-6-pending-updates-low.expected: Apply pending updates within the maintenance window
CM-6-pending-updates-low.why: A small backlog is normal but should not be allowed to grow. NIST 800-53 CM-6 / SI-2 require tracking and applying configuration and security updates. Catching this at single-digit count keeps the apply-and-test cycle cheap.
CM-6-pending-updates-low.fix: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade  # review first with: apt list --upgradable

CM-6-pending-updates-high.why: A large patch backlog usually means the host has missed at least one security advisory cycle. NIST 800-53 CM-6 / SI-2 require timely flaw remediation. Each unpatched package multiplies the chance that a published CVE is exploitable on this host.
CM-6-pending-updates-high.fix: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade  # for security-only: sudo unattended-upgrade -d

CM-7-risky-service-running.what: A service from the deny-list is currently running (telnet, rsh, rlogin, vsftpd, pure-ftpd, proftpd, xinetd, cups, avahi-daemon)
CM-7-risky-service-running.expected: Service stopped and disabled if not explicitly required
CM-7-risky-service-running.why: These services are commonly enabled by default but rarely needed on a server, and several (telnet, rsh, rlogin, plain FTP) transmit credentials in clear. NIST 800-53 CM-7 requires the system be configured to provide only essential capabilities. Each unused listener is an attack surface for no functional gain.
CM-7-risky-service-running.fix: sudo systemctl disable --now <service>  # confirm with whoever owns the host first

CM-7-risky-service-enabled.what: A risky service is enabled (will start at boot) even though not currently running
CM-7-risky-service-enabled.expected: Service disabled if not explicitly required
CM-7-risky-service-enabled.why: An enabled-but-stopped service is one reboot or one operator mistake away from active. NIST 800-53 CM-7 requires removing or disabling unused capabilities — leaving them enabled defers the risk rather than removing it. This is a common 'I'll fix it later' state that quietly persists.
CM-7-risky-service-enabled.fix: sudo systemctl disable <service>  # and consider: sudo apt purge <package>

CM-7-ssh-permit-root.expected: PermitRootLogin no  (or prohibit-password if key-only root is required)
CM-7-ssh-permit-root.why: Direct root SSH login means a single compromised credential or key is full system takeover, with no audit trail tying actions to a human operator. NIST 800-53 CM-7 (least functionality) and AC-6 (least privilege) both argue for forcing operators to log in as themselves and elevate via sudo. This also breaks brute-force attempts since 'root' is the universal target username.
CM-7-ssh-permit-root.fix: Run scripts/harden-ssh-macos.sh (macOS) or: sudo sed -i 's/^#\?PermitRootLogin.*/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config && sudo systemctl reload ssh (Linux)

CM-7-ssh-password-auth.what: SSH PasswordAuthentication is not explicitly disabled
CM-7-ssh-password-auth.why: Password-based SSH is vulnerable to brute force and credential stuffing in a way key-based auth is not. NIST 800-53 CM-7 plus IA-2 (identification & authentication) favor cryptographic authenticators over reusable passwords for remote access. Once you've migrated all operators to keys, this should be locked down.
CM-7-ssh-password-auth.fix: Run scripts/harden-ssh-macos.sh (macOS) or: sudo sed -i 's/^#\?PasswordAuthentication.*/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config && sudo systemctl reload ssh (Linux)  # confirm key-based login works first

IA-5-pass-max-days.what: PASS_MAX_DAYS in /etc/login.defs is unset or greater than 90
IA-5-pass-max-days.why: Without password-age caps, compromised credentials remain valid indefinitely. NIST 800-53 IA-5 requires periodic authenticator refresh. While modern guidance debates rotation frequency for human users, server policy still typically enforces a ceiling so that abandoned accounts can't accumulate stale, breached credentials.

IA-5-pass-min-days.why: PASS_MIN_DAYS=0 lets a user immediately rotate back to a previous password, defeating any history-based reuse policy. NIST 800-53 IA-5 expects password change controls to actually take effect. A non-zero minimum prevents the trivial 'change-then-change-back' bypass.

IA-5-pass-warn-age.what: PASS_WARN_AGE in /etc/login.defs is unset or below 7
IA-5-pass-warn-age.why: Without an expiry warning window, users are surprised by lockout and tend to pick low-effort replacement passwords under time pressure. NIST 800-53 IA-5 supports operationally workable authenticator management. A week of warning lets users plan a real password rotation.

IA-5-pwquality-minlen.what: pwquality minlen is unset or below 12
IA-5-pwquality-minlen.why: Short passwords are within reach of offline cracking against any leaked hash. NIST 800-53 IA-5 requires authenticators of sufficient strength for the protected resource. 12 characters is the contemporary floor for password complexity guidance against modern GPU-assisted cracking.

IA-5-pwquality-credit.expected: Credits configured to require character-class diversity
IA-5-pwquality-credit.why: Without character-class credits, pwquality only enforces length — users may pick all-lowercase strings. NIST 800-53 IA-5 calls for authenticator complexity appropriate to the threat. Class credits force at least some diversity even when minlen alone would pass.
IA-5-pwquality-credit.fix: Edit /etc/security/pwquality.conf and add: dcredit = -1, ucredit = -1, ocredit = -1, lcredit = -1

IA-5-pwquality-missing.why: No pwquality means no enforced complexity, length, or dictionary checks at password-set time. NIST 800-53 IA-5 requires authenticator strength enforcement at the point of creation, not just at the point of use. Without pwquality, weak passwords get accepted and only get caught later (if ever) by a breach.
IA-5-pwquality-missing.fix: sudo apt install libpam-pwquality  # then configure /etc/security/pwquality.conf

IA-2-faillock-not-configured.expected: pam_faillock entries present in PAM auth stack
IA-2-faillock-not-configured.why: Without account-lockout, brute-force attempts can run unbounded against console and SSH password logins. NIST 800-53 IA-2 expects the system to limit consecutive invalid login attempts. faillock is the standard Linux mechanism; without it, defense relies entirely on fail2ban or upstream rate limiting.

IA-2-faillock-deny.what: faillock deny threshold is unset or above 5
IA-2-faillock-deny.why: A high deny threshold allows many guesses before lockout, undermining the brute-force protection. NIST 800-53 IA-2 expects login-attempt limits sized to deter automated guessing. Five is a common balance between operator typos and attacker scripts.

IA-2-faillock-unlock-time.what: faillock unlock_time is unset or below 1800 seconds
IA-2-faillock-unlock-time.why: Short unlock windows let an attacker resume guessing minutes later, drastically extending the effective attempt budget. NIST 800-53 IA-2 expects lockout durations long enough to actually slow brute force. 30 minutes per lock keeps attacker throughput negligible while remaining recoverable for legitimate operator typos.

IA-2-faillock-conf-missing.what: pam_faillock referenced in PAM stack but /etc/security/faillock.conf is missing
IA-2-faillock-conf-missing.why: Without the conf file, faillock falls back to compiled defaults — defaults that vary across distributions and are hard to audit. NIST 800-53 IA-2 expects authenticator behavior to be documented and enforced. Drop the conf in place to make policy explicit.
IA-2-faillock-conf-missing.fix: sudo cp /usr/share/doc/libpam-modules/examples/faillock.conf /etc/security/faillock.conf  # or create from scratch with deny= and unlock_time= entries

IA-2-tmout-unset.what: TMOUT shell timeout not configured in /etc/profile or /etc/profile.d/
IA-2-tmout-unset.why: Idle interactive sessions left logged in are a session-hijacking risk if the operator walks away. NIST 800-53 IA-2 (specifically IA-2(8) — session lock) calls for terminating or locking sessions after a defined inactivity period. TMOUT is the bash-level mechanism; it complements display-locker policies for unattended terminals.

SC-8-cloudflared-not-detected.what: OpenClaw gateway is listening but cloudflared is not running
SC-8-cloudflared-not-detected.expected: cloudflared running (Cloudflare Tunnel TLS termination) or TLS configured directly on the gateway
SC-8-cloudflared-not-detected.why: Without TLS termination, gateway traffic is unencrypted on the wire, exposing tokens and agent payloads. NIST 800-53 SC-8 requires confidentiality of transmitted information. The OpenClaw deployment pattern relies on Cloudflare Tunnel for TLS; if cloudflared isn't running, either the tunnel is down or the gateway is exposed without TLS.
SC-8-cloudflared-not-detected.fix: sudo systemctl start cloudflared  # or: cloudflared tunnel run <tunnel-name>  # if direct access intended, configure TLS on the gateway and document

SC-28-config-perm.family: SC-28 — Protection of Information at Rest
SC-28-config-perm.why: config.json holds connection details and possibly inline credentials for the OpenClaw runtime. NIST 800-53 SC-28 requires protection of information at rest. World/group-readable config gives any local process a roadmap of services and their auth material.

SC-28-config-owner.family: SC-28 — Protection of Information at Rest
SC-28-config-owner.what: ~/.openclaw/config.json is owned by an unexpected user
SC-28-config-owner.expected: Owned by the current operator/service user
SC-28-config-owner.why: Wrong ownership often means the file was created by a different account (root via sudo, an installer, or a previous user) and may have unexpected permission semantics. NIST 800-53 SC-28 + AC-3 require explicit ownership of sensitive resources. Sort out who is supposed to own the config and align permissions.

SC-28-world-readable-secrets.family: SC-28 — Protection of Information at Rest
SC-28-world-readable-secrets.expected: No files in ~/.openclaw are world-readable (mode bit o+r unset)
SC-28-world-readable-secrets.why: World-readable files in the OpenClaw workspace expose agent state, memory snippets, and potentially cached credentials to any local user. NIST 800-53 SC-28 requires confidentiality at rest. Even non-secret workspace files can leak operational intelligence (agent names, schedules, target hostnames).
SC-28-world-readable-secrets.fix: chmod -R o-r ~/.openclaw  # then re-verify with: find ~/.openclaw -type f -perm /004

SI-2-security-updates-low.expected: Apply security updates within the maintenance window
SI-2-security-updates-low.why: Even a small number of pending security updates represents known-exploitable surface. NIST 800-53 SI-2 requires the organization to identify, report, and correct flaws in a timely manner. Catching this early keeps the apply-and-test cost low.
SI-2-security-updates-low.fix: sudo unattended-upgrade -d  # or: sudo apt upgrade  # for security-only the unattended path is preferred

SI-2-security-updates-high.why: A large security backlog means the host has likely missed at least one published advisory cycle. NIST 800-53 SI-2 requires timely flaw remediation; this is the canonical metric. Schedule the patch window now — every additional day extends the exposure to publicly known vulnerabilities.
SI-2-security-updates-high.fix: sudo apt update && sudo unattended-upgrade -d  # follow with: sudo reboot if kernel updates were included

SI-3-clamav-not-installed.expected: ClamAV (or another endpoint malware scanner) installed
SI-3-clamav-not-installed.why: Without an on-host malware scanner, attached files (downloads, email attachments, agent inputs) are unscreened. NIST 800-53 SI-3 requires malicious code protection. ClamAV is the conventional Linux choice; alternatives are acceptable but Sarge can only attest to ClamAV today.
SI-3-clamav-not-installed.fix: sudo apt install clamav clamav-daemon clamav-freshclam

SI-3-clamav-daemon-stopped.what: ClamAV is installed but clamav-daemon is not running
SI-3-clamav-daemon-stopped.why: An installed-but-stopped scanner provides no protection. NIST 800-53 SI-3 requires the protection mechanism to actually run. The daemon enables on-access scanning rather than only scheduled batch scans.

SI-3-freshclam-stopped.what: ClamAV signature updater (freshclam) is not running
SI-3-freshclam-stopped.expected: clamav-freshclam active to keep signatures current
SI-3-freshclam-stopped.why: ClamAV with stale signatures will miss everything published after the last update. NIST 800-53 SI-3 requires malicious code protection mechanisms to be kept current. freshclam handles automatic signature refresh; without it, your scanner ages out of usefulness within days.

SI-3-fail2ban-not-running.expected: fail2ban active with appropriate jails (sshd, etc.)
SI-3-fail2ban-not-running.why: fail2ban is the host-side rate-limiter for SSH and other auth daemons; without it, brute-force attempts run as fast as the network allows. NIST 800-53 SI-3 (and IA-2) expect controls against automated credential attacks. fail2ban complements faillock by acting at the network/process layer rather than only at PAM.
SI-3-fail2ban-not-running.fix: sudo apt install fail2ban && sudo systemctl enable --now fail2ban  # or run scripts/harden-fail2ban.sh

SI-7-checksum-mismatch.family: SI-7 — Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
SI-7-checksum-mismatch.expected: All files listed in CHECKSUMS.sha256 match their recorded hashes
SI-7-checksum-mismatch.why: A checksum mismatch means a sarge script has been modified since the checksum was recorded — either an authorized update (in which case the checksum file is stale) or unauthorized tampering. NIST 800-53 SI-7 requires integrity verification of system software. Sarge is itself a security tool; tampering with it could mute the very findings it should report.
SI-7-checksum-mismatch.fix: Re-clone the sarge repo from a trusted source, or if the change was intentional: regenerate checksums with: sha256sum scripts/*.sh assessment/**/*.sh > CHECKSUMS.sha256

WIN-AC-3-workspace-acl.what: OpenClaw workspace directory (%USERPROFILE%\.openclaw) ACL grants access to principals other than the owner, SYSTEM, and Administrators
WIN-AC-3-workspace-acl.expected: ACL restricted to owner + SYSTEM + Administrators (mirrors the Ubuntu 700 requirement)
WIN-AC-3-workspace-acl.why: Group/world-readable workspace exposes OpenClaw state and credential paths to other local users on the host. NIST 800-53 AC-3 requires per-resource access enforcement.

WIN-AC-6-admin-group.what: Local Administrators group has more than the owner + built-in Administrator member
WIN-AC-6-admin-group.expected: Two or fewer members on a workstation (built-in Administrator + one named admin)
WIN-AC-6-admin-group.why: Every Administrators member is a path to full local privilege escalation if their interactive session is compromised. NIST 800-53 AC-6 requires least-privilege scoping.

WIN-AC-7-lockout-threshold.what: Account lockout threshold is 0 (no lockout) or above 10
WIN-AC-7-lockout-threshold.why: Without a lockout policy, brute-force attempts against local accounts are unbounded. NIST 800-53 AC-7 requires the system to enforce a limit on consecutive invalid logon attempts.

WIN-AC-11-idle-lock.what: Screen saver timeout missing, exceeds 900s, or does not require re-auth on resume
WIN-AC-11-idle-lock.why: An unattended unlocked workstation is the simplest physical compromise path. NIST 800-53 AC-11 requires an automatic session lock after a period of inactivity.
WIN-AC-11-idle-lock.fix: Settings > Personalization > Lock screen > Screen saver: set wait to 15 min or less and enable 'On resume, display logon screen'.

WIN-AU-2-audit-policy.what: One or more required audit categories (Logon, Account Logon, Account Management, Policy Change, System Integrity) is set to No Auditing
WIN-AU-2-audit-policy.expected: All five required categories set to Success and Failure
WIN-AU-2-audit-policy.why: Without audit categories enabled, security-relevant events never reach the event log. NIST 800-53 AU-2 requires the organization to determine and enable specific event types for logging.

WIN-AU-4-log-retention.what: Core event log channel is sized below 64 MB
WIN-AU-4-log-retention.expected: Security, System, Application, and PowerShell/Operational sized 64 MB or larger
WIN-AU-4-log-retention.why: Small log buffers wrap quickly under attack, destroying evidence. NIST 800-53 AU-4 requires sufficient storage to retain audit records.
WIN-AU-4-log-retention.fix: wevtutil sl Security /ms:268435456   (256 MB; repeat per channel, elevated)

WIN-AU-9-security-log-acl.what: Security.evtx ACL grants access to principals beyond SYSTEM / Administrators / EventLog
WIN-AU-9-security-log-acl.expected: ACL restricted to those three principals (or unreadable to standard users entirely)
WIN-AU-9-security-log-acl.why: Tampering with audit records breaks chain-of-custody. NIST 800-53 AU-9 requires protection of audit information from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion.

WIN-AU-12-log-location.what: Informational - reports the file location of audit channels (not a FAIL signal on its own)
WIN-AU-12-log-location.why: Knowing the on-disk location is required for forensics-grade collection. NIST 800-53 AU-12 covers the generation surface; AU-11 covers retention.
WIN-AU-12-log-location.fix: No fix required if entries are present; preserve default paths.

WIN-CM-6-smbv1.why: SMBv1 is the EternalBlue surface and lacks modern signing/encryption. NIST 800-53 CM-6 requires established secure configuration settings.

WIN-CM-6-legacy-services.what: One or more legacy services (Telnet, TlntSvr, RemoteRegistry, SNMP, Browser, SharedAccess, seclogon) is in the Running state
WIN-CM-6-legacy-services.expected: All listed services Stopped and Startup=Disabled
WIN-CM-6-legacy-services.why: Legacy services expose unauthenticated or weakly-authenticated network surfaces. NIST 800-53 CM-6 mandates removal of unneeded functionality.

WIN-CM-7-unnecessary-services.what: A service typically disabled in the CIS L1 workstation profile is running
WIN-CM-7-unnecessary-services.why: Each running service is an attack surface. NIST 800-53 CM-7 requires the system to provide only essential capabilities.
WIN-CM-7-unnecessary-services.fix: Confirm intent; if not needed: Set-Service <name> -StartupType Disabled; Stop-Service <name>   (elevated)

WIN-CM-8-software-inventory.what: Informational - inventory of installed software gathered from Uninstall registry hive
WIN-CM-8-software-inventory.why: Knowing what is installed is the prerequisite for CM-8 / CM-10 inventory accuracy.
WIN-CM-8-software-inventory.fix: Review the inventory exported to %USERPROFILE%\.sarge\state\windows-software-inventory.json and remove unauthorized entries.

WIN-IA-2-account-types.family: IA-2 / AC-2 - Identification and Authentication / Account Management
WIN-IA-2-account-types.what: Microsoft Account (consumer MSA) is present on the host
WIN-IA-2-account-types.expected: Local + AAD-managed identities only; no MSA-attached profiles on accredited hosts
WIN-IA-2-account-types.why: MSAs are managed by Microsoft, not the organization. NIST 800-53 AC-2 requires demonstrable org control over account lifecycle (provisioning, de-provisioning, attribute management). An auditor in a Moderate/High baseline will write a finding. If the host is intentionally outside the accreditation boundary (e.g. personal dev laptop), treat as informational.
WIN-IA-2-account-types.fix: Settings > Accounts > Sign in with a local account instead, or convert to an AAD-joined identity managed by the org. If outside accreditation boundary, document the scope exclusion.

WIN-IA-5-password-policy.what: Local password policy below baseline (min length less than 14, history less than 5, or max age more than 365 days)
WIN-IA-5-password-policy.expected: min length 14, history 5, max age 90-365 days
WIN-IA-5-password-policy.why: Weak local password policy lets compromised credentials persist. NIST 800-53 IA-5 requires authenticator strength commensurate with risk.

WIN-IA-5-complexity.what: PasswordComplexity is not 1, or reversible encryption is enabled
WIN-IA-5-complexity.why: Reversible encryption stores effectively-plaintext credentials. Disabling complexity weakens brute-force resistance. NIST 800-53 IA-5 covers authenticator content.
WIN-IA-5-complexity.fix: secpol.msc > Account Policies > Password Policy: enable complexity; disable reversible encryption.

WIN-IA-11-reauth.why: Re-authentication after idle is what makes physical-presence binding meaningful. NIST 800-53 IA-11 requires re-auth after a period defined by the organization.
WIN-IA-11-reauth.fix: secpol.msc > Local Policies > Security Options > Interactive logon: Machine inactivity limit = 900

WIN-SC-7-firewall-profiles.what: One or more firewall profiles (Domain, Private, Public) is disabled
WIN-SC-7-firewall-profiles.why: A disabled profile means inbound traffic on that network category is uncontrolled. NIST 800-53 SC-7 requires boundary protection at the host.

WIN-SC-7-listening-ports.what: TCP listeners bound to 0.0.0.0 or :: are present
WIN-SC-7-listening-ports.expected: Each externally-bound listener is intentional and reviewed
WIN-SC-7-listening-ports.why: 0.0.0.0 / :: bindings are reachable from any interface, including hostile networks. NIST 800-53 SC-7 requires limiting external connectivity to documented services.
WIN-SC-7-listening-ports.fix: Get-NetTCPConnection -State Listen | Where-Object LocalAddress -in 0.0.0.0,:: | Format-Table   (review each; disable the owning service if not required)

WIN-SC-8-smb-signing.what: SMB signing not required on both client and server
WIN-SC-8-smb-signing.expected: RequireSecuritySignature=True on both Get-SmbServerConfiguration and Get-SmbClientConfiguration
WIN-SC-8-smb-signing.why: Without signing, SMB sessions are vulnerable to relay attacks. NIST 800-53 SC-8 requires cryptographic protection of transmissions.

WIN-SC-13-bitlocker.what: OS volume BitLocker protection status is not On
WIN-SC-13-bitlocker.expected: ProtectionStatus=On on the C: (or OS) volume
WIN-SC-13-bitlocker.why: An unencrypted OS volume leaks data the moment the device is lost or stolen. NIST 800-53 SC-13 requires the use of FIPS-validated cryptography to protect data.
WIN-SC-13-bitlocker.fix: manage-bde -on <drive> -RecoveryPassword   (elevated); or Settings > Privacy & Security > Device encryption.

WIN-SI-2-pending-updates.why: Unpatched flaws are the dominant initial-access vector. NIST 800-53 SI-2 requires timely remediation of identified flaws.
WIN-SI-2-pending-updates.fix: Settings > Windows Update > Check for updates; or Install-Module PSWindowsUpdate; Install-WindowsUpdate -AcceptAll

WIN-SI-3-defender-realtime.what: Defender realtime or antivirus protection is disabled
WIN-SI-3-defender-realtime.why: Without realtime AV, malicious code is detected only at scheduled scans, by which time impact is often complete. NIST 800-53 SI-3 requires malicious-code protection.
WIN-SI-3-defender-realtime.fix: Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $false   (elevated); confirm no conflicting third-party AV is registered.

WIN-SI-3-tamper-protection.why: Tamper protection prevents malware from disabling Defender via API/registry. Without it, the AV is a soft target. NIST 800-53 SI-3 requires the protection mechanism to itself be protected.
WIN-SI-3-tamper-protection.fix: Settings > Privacy & Security > Windows Security > Virus & threat protection > Manage settings > Tamper Protection: On.

WIN-SI-3-asr-rules.what: No Attack Surface Reduction rules are configured
WIN-SI-3-asr-rules.expected: ASR rule set deployed in Block or Audit mode (per organizational baseline)
WIN-SI-3-asr-rules.why: ASR rules block the most common malware behaviors (Office macros launching child processes, etc). NIST 800-53 SI-3 is well-served by ASR as a defense-in-depth control.
WIN-SI-3-asr-rules.fix: Configure ASR via Intune or: Set-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids <guid> -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions Enabled   (per rule, elevated)

WIN-SI-4-sysmon.expected: Sysmon installed and running with a published config policy
WIN-SI-4-sysmon.why: Sysmon improves SI-4 system monitoring posture by recording process creation, network connections, file/registry changes, and other security-relevant telemetry that the default Windows event log does not capture.
WIN-SI-4-sysmon.fix: Install Sysmon from https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/sysmon and configure with a published policy. Olaf Hartong's sysmon-modular (https://github.com/olafhartong/sysmon-modular) is a reasonable starting point.

WIN-SI-7-wdac-policy.family: SI-7 - Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
WIN-SI-7-wdac-policy.what: No WDAC policy files present and WDAC not in enforced state
WIN-SI-7-wdac-policy.expected: WDAC enforced or, at minimum, audit-mode policies present (per organizational baseline)
WIN-SI-7-wdac-policy.why: WDAC is the strongest application-control surface on Windows. NIST 800-53 SI-7 covers software integrity; WDAC enforces it at execution time.
WIN-SI-7-wdac-policy.fix: Deploy a WDAC policy via CITool or Intune. WDAC is optional but recommended for high-assurance hosts.

WIN-AC-8-legal-banner.what: Legal logon banner (LegalNoticeCaption and/or LegalNoticeText) is empty under HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System
WIN-AC-8-legal-banner.expected: Both LegalNoticeCaption and LegalNoticeText set to organization-approved language
WIN-AC-8-legal-banner.why: NIST 800-53 AC-8 requires the system to display a legal-use notification before granting access. Without it, an org loses the consent-to-monitor footing that supports later disciplinary or law-enforcement action against misuse.
WIN-AC-8-legal-banner.fix: secpol.msc > Local Policies > Security Options > Interactive logon: Message title/text for users attempting to log on. Or set the LegalNoticeCaption/LegalNoticeText registry values via GPO/Intune.

WIN-AC-12-session-termination.what: LanmanServer autodisconnect is -1 (never) or above 15 minutes
WIN-AC-12-session-termination.why: NIST 800-53 AC-12 requires the system to terminate user sessions after a defined idle threshold. An indefinite SMB session means a stolen workstation token keeps file-server access alive long after the operator walks away.
WIN-AC-12-session-termination.fix: Set HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters\autodisconnect to 15 via secpol.msc or registry (elevated). Restart LanmanServer.

WIN-AC-17-rdp-posture.what: RDP enabled without NLA (UserAuthentication=0), or below MinEncryptionLevel 3, or SecurityLayer below 2 (no TLS/CredSSP)
WIN-AC-17-rdp-posture.expected: RDP disabled, OR UserAuthentication=1, MinEncryptionLevel=3, SecurityLayer=2
WIN-AC-17-rdp-posture.why: NIST 800-53 AC-17 requires that remote access methods be authenticated and encrypted. RDP without NLA is exposed to credential-replay; legacy RDP encryption is vulnerable to downgrade and man-in-the-middle.
WIN-AC-17-rdp-posture.fix: Set-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\WinStations\RDP-Tcp' UserAuthentication 1; MinEncryptionLevel 3; SecurityLayer 2 (elevated). Or disable RDP entirely if not needed.

WIN-AU-3-sysmon-config.what: Sysmon is installed but no config rules are present in the service registry parameters
WIN-AU-3-sysmon-config.expected: Sysmon configured with a published policy (rules_bytes > 0)
WIN-AU-3-sysmon-config.why: NIST 800-53 AU-3 requires that audit records contain enough information to support analysis. Sysmon with no configuration emits only minimal event data, defeating the purpose of installing it. If Sysmon isn't installed, this control is informational only.
WIN-AU-3-sysmon-config.fix: Apply a Sysmon config: sysmon -accepteula -i <config.xml> (elevated). Olaf Hartong's sysmon-modular is a common starting point.

WIN-AU-6-event-forwarding.family: AU-6 - Audit Review, Analysis, and Reporting
WIN-AU-6-event-forwarding.what: No Windows Event Forwarding SubscriptionManager URL is configured on the host
WIN-AU-6-event-forwarding.expected: At least one collector URL under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\EventLog\EventForwarding\SubscriptionManager
WIN-AU-6-event-forwarding.why: NIST 800-53 AU-6 requires that audit records be reviewed and analyzed. A host with no central log collector (WEF or SIEM agent) silos its evidence on the host itself, where an attacker who gains local access can also tamper with the logs.
WIN-AU-6-event-forwarding.fix: Group Policy > Computer > Admin Templates > Windows Components > Event Forwarding > Configure target Subscription Manager. Or push the SubscriptionManager URL via Intune/registry.

WIN-AU-8-time-config.what: W32Time configured as NoSync (no time source), or the Type is unrecognized
WIN-AU-8-time-config.expected: Type=NTP / NT5DS / AllSync with a valid NtpServer pointer
WIN-AU-8-time-config.why: NIST 800-53 AU-8 requires that audit records carry accurate, comparable timestamps. A host without time sync drifts seconds-to-minutes per day, breaking correlation across hosts and undermining incident-response timelines.
WIN-AU-8-time-config.fix: w32tm /config /syncfromflags:manual /manualpeerlist:'time.windows.com,0x1' /update; net stop w32time && net start w32time (elevated).

WIN-CM-2-baseline-drift.what: Configuration drift detected vs the stored baseline (added/removed services, scheduled tasks, or policy-registry value changes)
WIN-CM-2-baseline-drift.expected: No drift, OR an intentional drift acknowledged by refreshing the baseline file
WIN-CM-2-baseline-drift.why: NIST 800-53 CM-2 requires maintaining a current baseline configuration. Silent drift in services, scheduled tasks, or policy registry keys often signals either unauthorized change or a planned change that escaped the change-control record.
WIN-CM-2-baseline-drift.fix: Review the diff in the finding message. If the change is intentional, refresh the baseline: Remove-Item $env:USERPROFILE\.sarge\state\windows-baseline.json; re-run assess.ps1.

WIN-CM-11-user-installed-software.what: Per-user AppX packages or HKCU uninstall entries detected without an enforcing policy
WIN-CM-11-user-installed-software.expected: Per-user installs allow-listed, OR Store / non-MSI installs blocked by AppLocker / WDAC / Group Policy
WIN-CM-11-user-installed-software.why: NIST 800-53 CM-11 requires the organization to govern what software users may install. Per-user installs bypass machine-wide change control and inventory; without an allow-list (or a deny-all + exception list), there's no enforcement surface for the control.
WIN-CM-11-user-installed-software.fix: Configure an AppLocker / WDAC publisher allow-list, or disable the Store via Group Policy: Computer > Admin Templates > Windows Components > Store > Turn off the Store application.

WIN-IA-3-device-id.what: TPM or Secure Boot is not present / not enabled on the host
WIN-IA-3-device-id.expected: TPM present and Ready=True; SecureBoot enabled
WIN-IA-3-device-id.why: NIST 800-53 IA-3 requires that devices be identified and authenticated before being granted network or service access. Modern attestation and credential-binding flows rely on a usable TPM and a Secure Boot chain; without them, device identity cannot be cryptographically asserted.
WIN-IA-3-device-id.fix: Enable TPM and Secure Boot in firmware; ensure Windows can talk to the TPM (Get-Tpm | TpmPresent True / TpmReady True). On hosts lacking TPM hardware, this control is UNTESTED.

WIN-IA-12-windows-hello.what: Windows Hello (NGC) keys not present for any user profile on the host
WIN-IA-12-windows-hello.expected: At least one user has a Windows Hello credential, OR identity proofing is performed centrally (AAD + MFA) and Hello is intentionally not deployed
WIN-IA-12-windows-hello.why: NIST 800-53 IA-12 calls for identity proofing prior to authenticator issuance. Windows Hello biometrics / PIN binds proof to the local device, supporting phishing-resistant logon. Absence of NGC keys means all logins rely on reusable passwords.
WIN-IA-12-windows-hello.fix: Settings > Accounts > Sign-in options > Windows Hello PIN / Face / Fingerprint. For managed hosts, deploy via Intune: Endpoint Security > Account protection.

WIN-SC-2-uac.family: SC-2 - Separation of System and User Functionality
WIN-SC-2-uac.what: UAC disabled (EnableLUA=0), or ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin set to 0 (no prompt)
WIN-SC-2-uac.expected: EnableLUA=1; ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin >= 2 (prompt for consent on secure desktop)
WIN-SC-2-uac.why: NIST 800-53 SC-2 requires functional separation between privileged and unprivileged user contexts. UAC is the Windows enforcement mechanism: disabling it collapses the admin/user boundary and makes every code-execution-as-user a code-execution-as-admin.

WIN-SC-12-key-mgmt.family: SC-12 - Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
WIN-SC-12-key-mgmt.what: LSA Protection (RunAsPPL) disabled and/or Credential Guard not running
WIN-SC-12-key-mgmt.why: NIST 800-53 SC-12 requires protection of credentials and keys handled by the system. LSASS holds secrets in process memory; LSA Protection (PPL) and Credential Guard isolate that memory from non-PPL code, blocking the standard Mimikatz lsass.exe extraction path.
WIN-SC-12-key-mgmt.fix: reg add HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa /v RunAsPPL /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f; enable Credential Guard via Group Policy > Computer > Admin Templates > System > Device Guard > Turn On Virtualization Based Security (elevated, reboot).

WIN-SC-23-ldap-ntlm.what: LDAPServerIntegrity below 2 (signing not required) and/or NTLM outbound traffic not restricted
WIN-SC-23-ldap-ntlm.why: NIST 800-53 SC-23 requires session authenticity for sensitive protocols. Unsigned LDAP and unrestricted NTLM are the primary surfaces for relay attacks; signing and NTLM restriction defeat the common kerberoasting / NTLM-relay paths.
WIN-SC-23-ldap-ntlm.fix: secpol.msc > Local Policies > Security Options > Domain controller: LDAP server signing requirements = Require signing; Network security: Restrict NTLM: Outgoing NTLM traffic to remote servers = Audit or Deny.

WIN-SC-28-bitlocker-policy.family: SC-28 - Protection of Information at Rest
WIN-SC-28-bitlocker-policy.what: BitLocker is on but encryption method is below XtsAes128 or no recovery-key escrow location is configured
WIN-SC-28-bitlocker-policy.expected: EncryptionMethod >= XtsAes128 and a recovery-key escrow target (AD/AAD) configured by policy
WIN-SC-28-bitlocker-policy.why: NIST 800-53 SC-28 requires that at-rest data be protected with cryptography appropriate to the threat, and that key material survive operator turnover. Older BitLocker encryption methods (AES128 CBC) and missing escrow leave gaps.
WIN-SC-28-bitlocker-policy.fix: manage-bde -off C: && manage-bde -on C: -EncryptionMethod XtsAes256 -SkipHardwareTest (elevated). Configure recovery key escrow to Active Directory or Azure AD via Group Policy / Intune.

WIN-SI-5-update-reporting.family: SI-5 - Security Alerts, Advisories, and Directives
WIN-SI-5-update-reporting.what: WSUS status reporting server not configured (WUStatusServer registry value empty) AND Defender SubmitSamplesConsent is set to 0 (Never)
WIN-SI-5-update-reporting.expected: WSUS status server set, OR Defender sample submission enabled (>=1) so the host reports security events to a central pipeline
WIN-SI-5-update-reporting.why: NIST 800-53 SI-5 requires the org to receive and act on security alerts and advisories. A host that doesn't report patch status to WSUS and doesn't share Defender telemetry leaves the org blind to its security posture for this asset.
WIN-SI-5-update-reporting.fix: Set WSUS reporting via Group Policy: Computer > Admin Templates > Windows Components > Windows Update > Specify intranet Microsoft update service location. Or Set-MpPreference -SubmitSamplesConsent 1 (elevated).

WIN-SI-8-spam-protection.what: No mail role detected on the host (Exchange / IIS SMTP / Postfix-for-Windows not present)
WIN-SI-8-spam-protection.expected: Informational - SI-8 applies to mail-handling roles, not generic workstations
WIN-SI-8-spam-protection.why: NIST 800-53 SI-8 governs spam / phish protection for mail-handling components. On a typical OpenClaw host with no mail role, this control is N/A and is emitted informationally so the audit trail records the determination explicitly.
WIN-SI-8-spam-protection.fix: No action required for non-mail hosts. If a mail role is added later, deploy mail-server-side spam protection.

WIN-SI-16-memory-protection.what: System-wide ProcessMitigation policy missing one or more of: DEP, ASLR, CFG, mandatory image signing
WIN-SI-16-memory-protection.expected: DEP Enable=ON; ASLR ForceRelocateImages=ON; CFG Enable=ON; AuditCodeIntegrity / EnforceCodeIntegrity=ON
WIN-SI-16-memory-protection.why: NIST 800-53 SI-16 requires the system to implement memory-protection mechanisms. DEP, ASLR, CFG, and image-signing enforcement are the Windows surface; missing any of them weakens defense-in-depth against memory-corruption exploits.
WIN-SI-16-memory-protection.fix: Set-ProcessMitigation -System -Enable DEP,ForceRelocateImages,CFG (elevated). Review Get-ProcessMitigation -System for the full set.

WIN-POL-1.what: Host is AAD-joined but not enrolled in any MDM (Intune); MdmUrl is empty in dsregcmd /status output
WIN-POL-1.expected: AAD-joined hosts are enrolled in an MDM under the same tenant so configuration baselines are centrally enforced and drift can be remediated
WIN-POL-1.why: Azure AD join alone provides identity but no configuration enforcement. NIST 800-53 CM-2 requires a documented, enforced baseline; without MDM, the local registry is the only enforcement surface, so any local admin can drift the baseline silently. Sarge has no source of truth to overlay onto controls and cannot tell whether a local registry value reflects intent or accidental drift.
WIN-POL-1.fix: Enroll the device in Microsoft Intune (or another MDM) under the same AAD tenant. Settings > Accounts > Access work or school > Connect; or push enrollment via Conditional Access compliance policy. Tracked: oscarsixsecllc/sarge#31

WIN-POL-2.what: Managed-policy inventory captured (MDM CSP registry, gpresult HTML, and/or Get-GPO) and written to the run folder as policy-inventory.json
WIN-POL-2.expected: Informational - this finding always passes when --inspect-policy successfully gathered any policy data; the side-output JSON is the artifact
WIN-POL-2.why: Capturing the applied-policy inventory is the precondition for the verdict-overlay in Phase 1b. The JSON document is also useful for audit evidence under CM-6 (record of configuration settings actually applied to the system).
WIN-POL-2.fix: Not applicable - informational only. Inspect policy-inventory.json in the run folder for the captured settings.
